coredata.dc:title: Hydrothermal synthesis of highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots with good biocompatibility and the application for sensing ellagic acid
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Yongming
Zhao, Wei
coredata.dc:description: Blue emissive nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with a high quantum yield as high as 84.79% were successfully synthesized via the hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and diethylenetriamine in one pot. The as-prepared N-CQDs displayed excellent stability in high-salt conditions, good photostability, promising the N-CQDs as potential probes for selectively detecting ellagic acid with a linear range of 0.01–50 μM on the basis of inner filter effect. And the hydroponics experiment of gardenia with N-CQDs suggested the good biocompatibility of the N-CQDs, indicating the potential applications in biomedical fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Synthesis, applications
Detection
Ellagic acid

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Hydroponics experiment of gardenia with N-CQDs
Selectivity of N-CQDs for sensing EA

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Startup Foundation for Introducing Talent of Nanjing University of Information Science and Technology

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of hydrothermal synthesis of N-CQDs for sensing EA and the hydroponics experiment results of gardenia with N-CQDs.
(A) XPS survey spectrum of N-CQDs. (B) FT-IR spectrum of N-CQDs.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectrum of N-CQDs, inset: pictures of diluted N-CQDs solution upon irradiation of visible light (left) and 365 nm light (right). (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CQDs at different excitation wavelengths.

(A) The effect NaCl on the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs. (B) Photostability study of N-CQDs via continuous irradiation of 365 nm UV light (condition: pH 8.0, 50.0 mM phosphate buffer, λex = 350 nm).

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CQDs in the presence of different concentrations of EA. (B) The plot of fluorescence intensity ratio I/I0 versus the concentration of EA, inset: the linear plot of fluorescence intensity ratio I/I0 versus the EA concentration (condition: pH 8.0, 50.0 mM phosphate buffer, λex = 350 nm).

(A) Normalized UV–vis absorption spectrum of EA, normalized fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of N-CQDs. (B) Selectivity of N-CQDs towards EA over other interferences (condition: pH 8.0, 50.0 mM phosphate buffer, [EA] = 100 μM, [Interferences] = 1 mM, λex = 350 nm).

Hydroponics experiment results of gardenia: photographs of gardenia before (A and B) and after (C and D) incubation with different concentrations of N-CQDs for about 12 h under visible light without (A and C) and 365 nm UV light (B and D).

head.ce:title: Hydrothermal synthesis of highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots with good biocompatibility and the application for sensing ellagic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Reading Academy, NUIST-UoR International Research Institute, Institute of Advanced Materials and Flexible Electronics (IAMFE), School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Nanjing University of Information Science & Technology, Nanjing 210044, China

Institute of Advanced Materials and Flexible Electronics (IAMFE)
School of Chemistry and Materials Science
Nanjing University of Information Science & Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Engineering, Nanyang Normal University, Nanyang 473061, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Engineering, Nanyang Normal University, Nanyang 473061, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Blue emissive nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with a high quantum yield as high as 84.79% were successfully synthesized via the hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and diethylenetriamine in one pot. The as-prepared N-CQDs displayed excellent stability in high-salt conditions, good photostability, promising the N-CQDs as potential probes for selectively detecting ellagic acid with a linear range of 0.01–50 μM on the basis of inner filter effect. And the hydroponics experiment of gardenia with N-CQDs suggested the good biocompatibility of the N-CQDs, indicating the potential applications in biomedical fields.

Blue emissive nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with a high quantum yield of 84.79% were successfully synthesized, and the as-prepared N-CQDs were utilized to selectively detect ellagic acid and with a linear range of 0.01–50 μM on the basis of inner filter effect. And the hydroponics experiment of gardenia with N-CQDs suggested the good biocompatibility of the N-CQDs.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Ellagic acid (EA), a kind of polyphenolic phytonutrient, was commonly observed in lots of fruits and utilized in cosmetic products due to the antioxidant activity [1,2]. And EA was also utilized as promising protectors against many human diseases including cardiovascular disease and arthritis [3,4]. Moreover, EA was also observed to exhibit some toxic impacts on human health, and long-term consumption of EA may induce congestion in some organs [5]. Thus, simple and sensitive detection of EA is of great significance. Up to now, a variety of approaches have been developed for determining EA, such as chromatography [6,7], colorimetric method [8], fluorescence method [9,10], electrochemical method [11] and so on. However, some of these assays involve complicated operations, costly reagents and expensive instruments. Therefore, low-cost and simple assay of EA is highly desirable.

Over the past decades, fluorescent nanomaterials have been aroused tremendous interest due to their unique optical properties and considerable applications in various fields [12–17]. In particular, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted much attention owing to their distinct properties, including low-cost, ease of fabrication, bright fluorescence, high photostability, excellent biocompatibility, high quantum yield (QY) and good water solubility [15–17]. All of these properties promise CQDs to be widely utilized in a variety of fields including catalysis, biomedicine, sensing, optoelectronic devices and so on, demonstrating that their potential for replacing traditional organic dyes and heavy metal-doped quantum dots [12,13,17]. Consequently, a number of approaches have been constructed for synthesizing various CQDs, such as laser ablation, electrochemical method, thermal treatment, solvothermal method, hydrothermal method and so on [17]. Among these synthetic strategies, hydrothermal method, a convenient, low-cost and facile approach for preparing a variety of CQDs, is very popular.

In this paper, blue-emissive nitrogen-doped CQDs (N-CQDs) with a QY of as high as 84.79% and good biocompatibility were fabricated via hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and diethylenetriamine at 180 °C. The as-prepared N-CQDs was also characterized by ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Furthermore, the N-CQDs were constructed into a fluorescence turn-off nanosensor for detecting EA based on inner filter effect (IFE). Moreover, the hydroponics experiment results of gardenia with N-CQDs were utilized to investigate their toxicity (Fig. 1 ), revealing the excellent biocompatibility of the N-CQDs.

Citric acid (CA), Ellagic acid, diethylenetriamine, ascorbic acid, glutamic acid, cysteine, glutathione, glycine, DL-malic acid, kojic acid, mandelic acid, tranexamic acid, salicylic acid, arbutin, KCl, NaCl, CaCl2, MgCl2 and other reagents were of analytical reagent grade and utilized as received. Double distilled water was used throughout the experiment.

UV–vis absorption spectra were performed on a UV–vis spectrometer (Shimadzu UV-3600, Japan). Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra were measured on a Cary Eclipse luminescence spectrometer (Varian, USA). FT-IR spectra were carried on a Nicolet 5700 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo-Electron Corp., USA) with the KBr pellet technique. XPS spectra were obtained from an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALAB 250Xi, Thermo Scientific). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were carried on a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope. Fluorescence lifetime was gained from a FLS 980 spectrofluorimeter (Edinburgh Instrument, UK). Photostability tests were performed at room temperature using the light of 365 nm from an ultraviolet lamp (16 W, CBIO-UV3A, Beijing CBIO Bioscience Technology Co. Ltd., Beijing, China). And photographs were obtained using a Canon 700D digital camera.

The QY of N-CQDs was calculated according to a typical slope method by using quinine sulfate dihydrate (QY = 54% in 0.10 M sulfuric acid solution) as the reference according to the following equation [18]:
QY
N − CQDs
=
QY re
I
N − CQDs
/
I re
n
N − CQDs
/
n re
where I is the slope measured from the curves of the integral area of fluorescence emission spectra of N-CQDs and quinine sulfate under the excitation of 350 nm UV light versus absorbance, and n is the refractive index of solvents. The subscripts “N-CQDs” and “re” refer to the N-CQDs and reference, respectively. In the present experiment solutions, nN-CQDs / nre = 1.

The N-CQDs were prepared through a hydrothermal method [16]. In the present work, 5 mmol of CA was dissolved in 50 mL of distilled H2O, and then 10 mmol of diethylenetriamine was dropped into the above solution under magnetic stirring. The mixture was transferred into Teflon-lined autoclave, which was put into an oven with 180 °C for 10 h. Subsequently, the autoclave was taken out for cooling to room temperature. The obtained N-CQDs solution was further purified via a dialysis tube (500 Da) and preserved at 4 °C in refrigerator before used in further experiments. And the concentration of the purified N-CQDs solution was calculated to be about 24.7 g/L by weighing the dried N-CQDs from 1 mL of N-CQDs solution.

For the determination of EA, 30 μL of N-CQDs, 750 μL phosphate buffer (pH 8.0, 200 mM) and different concentrations of EA solution were mixed together. And water was used to dilute the mixture into 3.0 mL. After incubation for about 10 min at room temperature, the detection solutions were transferred into a quartz cuvette with thickness of 1 cm and the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded with the excitation wavelength at 350 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydroponics experiment of gardenia with N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fresh gardenias with bud were collected in the campus and put into 10 mL of distilled water, 10 mL of N-CQDs solution with the concentration of 12.35 and 24.7 g/L, respectively. After incubation for about 12 h in dark, the growth status of gardenias was recorded.

In this work, the N-CQDs were fabricated through the hydrothermal treatment of CA and diethylenetriamine at 180 °C. In the formation procedure, CA with many carboxyl groups to react with many amino groups of diethylenetriamine to generate small N-CODs at 180 °C. As time goes on, small N-CQDs gradually grew into bright N-CQDs [19,20]. The as-prepared N-CQDs were highly dispersible in water due to the presence of the amino groups, carboxyl groups and hydroxyl groups on the surface of N-CQDs. Also the N-CQDs were further purified with a dialysis tube (500 Da). The chemical compositions of the N-CQDs were characterized by XPS. The XPS survey spectrum of N-CQDs manifested three peaks at about 285 eV, 400 eV and 531 eV, which were attributed to C 1s, N 1s and O 1s signals (Fig. 2A), revealing that the N-CQDs mainly contained carbon, nitrogen and oxygen. In the high-resolution spectra, the C 1s spectrum was divided into three peaks at about 284.7 eV, 285.9 eV and 287.6 eV (Fig. S1A), which may be corresponding to C-C/C=C/C-H, CN and CO, respectively [21–23]. The N 1s XPS spectrum showed two peaks at about 399.3 and 401.1 eV (Fig. S1B), which may be assigned to NH bond and C-N/N-N bond [24]. The O 1s XPS spectrum could be fitted into two peaks at about 530.8 and 531.7 eV (Fig. S1C), representing the presence of O=C-NH and CO [25,26]. Obviously, the XPS results revealed that the N-CQDs contained many functional groups, such as NH2, O=C-NH and CO. FT-IR was further utilized to identify surface functional groups of the N-CQDs. The peak at about 3400 cm−1 could be attributed to the NH symmetric stretching [27,28]. The characteristic absorption peak at 2926 cm−1 corresponded to aliphatic CH2 asymmetric stretching modes [29]. And the peak observed at 1660 cm−1 could be ascribed to amides [30]. The peaks at 1260 cm−1 could be further attributed to the stretch vibrations of amides [31]. The peak at 1085 cm−1 indicated the presence of -CH-OH [32]. The absorption band at 795 cm−1 could be assigned the CH out-of-plane bending vibrations (Fig. 2B) [33]. The FT-IR results further confirmed the results of XPS. We also tried for many times to characterize the morphology of N-CQDs using TEM technique, an image was successfully obtained, the TEM image suggested the size of the N-CQDs was approximately 3.16 nm (Fig. S2).

The diluted N-CQDs solution displayed colorless in daylight, nevertheless, bright blue fluorescence was observed under 365 nm UV light, confirming the presence of N-CQDs (Fig. 2A). In the UV–vis absorption spectrum, two absorption peaks at about 240 nm and 350 nm were noticed (Fig. 3A), which are corresponding to the excitation spectrum of N-CQDs (Fig. S3). Consequently, 350 nm was selected as the maximum excitation wavelength for the following experiments. Fig. 3B displayed the fluorescence emission spectra excited at different wavelengths. When the excitation wavelength increased from 300 nm to 400 nm, the emission peak of N-CQDs showed a little red-shift, and the fluorescence intensity ascended firstly and then declined gradually. The excitation-dependent behavior may be attributed to the presence of inhomogeneous chemical structure, abundant trap states and non-uniform size distribution of N-CQDs [34]. Especially for non-uniform size distribution of N-CQDs, different size of N-CQDs may exhibit different emissions owing to the quantum size effect [35,36].

And the maximum emission wavelength was centered at about 440 nm when the N-CQDs were excited at 350 nm. Fluorescence decay technique demonstrated the fluorescence lifetime of N-CQDs was about 13.8001 ns, which is similar to that of most of CQDs (Fig. S4) [16,20]. The relative QY of N-CDs was determined to be about as high as 84.79% under 350 nm excitation using quinine sulfate as the standard (Fig. S5), [16,18,37] which is higher than the newly reported N-CQDs [38–42].

The effect of ionic strength on the fluorescence of N-CQDs was further investigated using NaCl solution. As shown in Fig. 4A, the fluorescence intensity at 440 nm was little influenced by NaCl, even though the concentration of NaCl was as high as 1.0 M, indicating the excellent stability of the N-CQDs in high salt conditions. The photostability of the N-CQDs was also studied through continuous irradiation of 365 nm UV light. The ratio of fluorescence intensity at 440 nm remained stable after continuous illumination of 10 min, revealing the good photostability of N-CQDs (Fig. 4B). The pH value of the N-CQDs solution may also affect the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs. We studied the fluorescence response of N-CQDs in solution with pH value from 3 to 12. As could be seen in Fig. S6, the fluorescence intensity of the N-CQDs was greatly dependent on pH value of the solution. With increment of pH value from 3 to 8, the fluorescence intensity at 440 nm increased gradually, however, when pH value further increased from 8 to 12, the fluorescence intensity declined slowly, indicating the optimum pH value was 8 (Fig. S6). The pH-dependent behavior may be attributed to the presence of both carboxyl groups and amino groups on the surface of N-CQDs. At pH < 8, the amino and carboxyl groups were easily protonated. And these groups were deprotonated again at pH > 8. When at pH = 8, the surface state of N-CQDs was optimum to emit bright fluorescence. Therefore, we chose pH = 8 as the optimum detection conditions.

Subsequently, we tried to detect EA by utilizing N-CQDs via fluorescence turn-off modes. We firstly investigated the effect of incubation time on the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs in the presence of EA. Upon adding 100 μM of EA, the fluorescence intensity at 440 nm (I440) sharply decreased and remained constant even the incubation time was extended to 30 min, indicating the rapid response rate (Fig. S7). With increasing the concentration of EA, the fluorescence emission peak intensity at 440 nm of N-CQDs reduced gradually, and the shape of emission peak was not changed (Fig. 5A). And the plot of fluorescence intensity ratio versus the concentration of EA was achieved. A good correlation linearity between the fluorescence intensity ratio and the concentration of EA was observed in the range from 0.01 μM to 50 μM with a correlation coefficient of 0.9968 (Fig. 5B), and the lowest detection concentration for EA was as low as 0.01 μM, which was better than other previous reports (Table S1) [8,9,43], confirming that the proposed method could be utilized to detect EA.

To explain the detection mechanism, we further investigated the UV–vis absorption spectrum of EA, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of N-CQDs, and their corresponding curves were put together. It was clearly observed that the UV–vis absorption spectrum of EA completely overlapped with the fluorescence excitation spectrum of N-CQDs, and partially superimposed with the emission spectrum of N-CQDs, verifying that IFE contributes to the fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs caused by EA. Specifically, EA could completely adsorb the excitation light and partially adsorb the fluorescence of N-CQDs, leading to the fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs (Fig. 6A) [44]. Furthermore, we recorded the fluorescence lifetime of N-CQDs in the absence and presence of EA to confirm the detection mechanism of EA under excitation of 350 nm. It was easily observed that the addition of EA did not alter the fluorescence lifetime of N-CQDs, revealing that the EA-caused fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs was attributed to the static quenching effect (Fig. S8). The static quenching effect suggested the fluorescence quenching was indeed caused by IFE more than fluorescence resonance energy transfer process [45,46].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of N-CQDs for sensing EA
ce:section.ce:para: In the real samples, there are often many substances, which may interfere with the detection of EA. Thus, citric acid (CA), vitamin C (Vc), glutamic acid (Glu), glycine (Gly), cysteine (Cys), glutathione (Glu), DL-malic acid (MA), kojic acid (KA), mandelic acid (MDA), tranexamic acid (TA), salicylic acid (SA), arbutin (AB) and three kinds of metal ions including Ca2+, Mg2+ and K+ were employed to evaluate the selectivity of the proposed method for EA detection. And the concentration of these interferences was 1 mM. As indicated in Fig. 6B, these interferences except for KA showed little effect on the detection of EA. And we all know that KA is a fungal metabolite commonly found in the production of a number of foodstuffs [47]. Thus, the as-prepared N-CQDs could be utilized to detect EA in samples without KA. The good selectivity of N-CQDs for EA may be due to the specific interactions between N-CQDs and EA. The amino groups on the surface of N-CQDs may specifically interact with the carboxyl groups of EA via hydrogen bonds, bringing them closer together and further resulting in fluorescence quenching.

The above results revealed that the developed method could be directly utilized to determine EA in practical samples. Thus, we assessed the feasibility of the proposed approach for sensing EA in urine. As indicated in Fig. S9, the diluted urine exhibited little effect on the fluorescence emission spectrum of N-CQDs, suggesting that the content of EA in the diluted urine was less than the lowest concentration of the proposed method. And the diluted urine was further spiked with EA to simulate the real samples. The detection results of EA in urine were showed in Table S2. The recoveries of known amount of EA in urine were good with a RSD <10%, demonstrating that the N-CQDs could be applied for detecting EA in real samples.

To investigate the biocompatibility of the as-prepared N-CQDs, we conducted the hydroponics experiment of gardenia with different concentrations of N-CQDs. After incubation for 12 h at room temperature, the gardenia without N-CQDs wilted, and the leaves softened. However, these gardenias incubated with N-CQDs were still in full bloom (Fig. 7A and C), and their flower and leaves displayed blue emission under 365 nm UV light, suggesting that the as-prepared N-CQDs were easily taken into gardenias and non-toxic to gardenias, demonstrating the good biocompatibility of the N-CQDs (Fig. 7B and D).

In conclusion, we have prepared blue-emissive N-CQDs via the hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and diethylenetriamine at 180 °C. The obtained N-CQDs exhibited a QY as high as 84.79%, good photostability and excellent stability in high-salt conditions. And the N-CQDs were successfully utilized to detect EA with good sensitivity and selectivity in light of IFE. Under the optimum conditions, a good linearity with the EA concentration in the range of 0.01–50 μM was obtained. And the hydroponics experiment of gardenia with N-CQDs revealed that the good biocompatibility of N-CQDs, suggesting their promising applications in various fields.

Yongming Guo: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Wei Zhao: Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was financially supported by the Foundation of NUIST-UoR International Research Institute, the Startup Foundation for Introducing Talent of NUIST (2018r056).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SSupplementary material: XPS spectra of C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, TEM image of N-CQDs, fluorescence excitation spectrum of N-CQDs, fluorescence lifetime test of N-CQDs, plot of integrated PL intensity of N-CQDs, fluorescence response of N-CQDs in different pH, effect of incubation time on the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs, comparison of EA detection methods, fluorescence decay curves of N-CQDs in the presence of EA, fluorescence emission spectra of N-CQDs in the presence of urine, analysis of EA (μM) in spiked human urine

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2020.118580.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Extraction and determination of ellagic acid contentin chestnut bark and fruit
Food Chem.

J. Chromatogr. A
